svg.text: what gets compared, transformed, and modified when a "update every Nth row"-style click fires

g.text: array refs r1..r5 point at row objects. live DOM mirrors them. each()'s WeakMap cache is warm from the previous render.
② handler builds a new array; only changed rows get fresh objects
immutable-update style. r1 and r3 are replaced with new objects (r6/r7); r2/r4/r5 keep their references. signal write triggers mount()'s effect.
const next = rows.value.slice(); for (let i=0; i<next.length; i+=2) next[i] = { ...next[i], label: next[i].label + ' !!!' }; rows.value = next;
③ each() walks the array and consults its WeakMap cache by item identity
cache hits return the previous render's HTML string verbatim. cache misses re-run render(item) and store the result.

svg.text: concatenate all 5 chunks → one SafeHtml string

g.text: ④ the table's body becomes one HTML string — fresh + cached chunks concatenated in order
render() returns a single SafeHtml. each row is a contiguous slice of the string. nothing is structured per-row at this layer.
⑤ innerHTML reparses the entire string into a detached DOM tree
the browser builds 5 fresh <tr> nodes (and all their children) regardless of whether their content matched. this cost is paid every render.

svg.text: morphdom walks both trees, matched by data-key

g.text: ⑥ morphdom pairs new ↔ live by data-key and decides per row: skip vs patch
isEqualNode short-circuits unchanged rows. changed rows fall through to the actual DOM patch (text-node update, attribute set, etc.).

svg.text: two text-node patches applied; live DOM matches new data

g.text: ⑦ live DOM after the morph
two <tr> subtrees received text-node updates. the other three are physically the same nodes they were before — not even touched.
